metadata.summary: Choose diff-only AI reviewers for repos under 100k LOC with strong isolation; choose graph-indexed for legacy/polyglot codebases over 500k LOC where cross-module ripple breaks diff-only coverage.

content.title: Graph-Indexed vs Diff-Only AI Reviewers
content.summary: Two architectures of AI code reviewers co-exist and behave differently. Diff-only reviewers (Sourcery, GitHub Copilot Code Review, Codeball) read the patch plus its nearest neighbours — fast, cheap, blind to cross-file impact. Graph-indexed reviewers (Greptile, CodeRabbit Pro, Qodo Merge 2.0 multi-agent) build a repo-wide knowledge graph and trace ripple effects — slower, expensive, catch cross-module breakage that the diff-only class misses entirely. Pick diff-only for monorepos with strong module isolation and <100k LOC; pick graph-indexed for legacy/polyglot codebases >500k LOC where one rename quietly breaks five callers.

why.p: Defaulting to "whatever the marketing page suggests" produces predictable failure: graph-indexed reviewers on a green-field 30k-LOC service slow PRs by minutes for negligible find rate, and diff-only reviewers on a 1M-LOC legacy monolith ship patches that break a downstream consumer in another package. The architecture choice is the dominant cost-vs-coverage knob; the rule below is a decision tree against measurable repo properties (LOC, module isolation, polyglot count, latency target) instead of vendor preference.

list.item: Selecting an AI reviewer for a new repo or migrating from a basic comment bot.
Re-evaluating after a repo crosses a size or polyglot threshold (e.g. acquisition, framework migration).
Auditing an existing reviewer that "feels noisy" or "missed an obvious break" — usually wrong architecture.
Cost-control reviews where the reviewer line item exceeds the budget for actual incident-prevention value.
One-off open-source contribution flow where the maintainer reads every PR manually anyway.
Repos with strict zero-third-party-app policy on source — pick a self-hosted PR-Agent variant or skip entirely.
Tiny PR pipelines (<5 PRs/day) — the per-PR cost difference is negligible; pick on UX, not architecture.

section.name: architecture-decision
section.p: The architectures have different cost curves. Diff-only is O(diff) — fast (<60s), cheap, blind beyond two hops. Graph-indexed is O(repo) on first index, then O(diff × graph-fanout) per PR — slow (minutes), expensive, catches cross-module ripple.

rule.statement: Pick `graph-indexed` when ANY hold: `LOC > 500_000`, `polyglot_languages > 2`, `module_boundaries == weak`, OR `bug_class == "innocent rename broke downstream"`. Pick `diff-only` when ALL hold: `LOC < 100_000`, `module_boundaries == strong`, `pr_feedback_target_seconds < 60`. For everything in between, default to `diff-only` (cheap baseline) plus on-demand `/review` from a graph-indexed bot for risky PRs.
rule.rationale: Greptile and CodeRabbit's own case studies converge on the LOC/polyglot threshold; Sourcery and Codeball published 60s feedback as their target, which only diff-only meets. Below 100k LOC the graph adds latency without proportionate find rate.

example.title: 1.4M LOC polyglot — graph-indexed choice
example.code: # .github/reviewer-decision.yml — repo profile drives choice repo: loc: 1_400_000 languages: [typescript, python, go, kotlin] module_boundaries: weak # legacy monolith pr_feedback_target_s: 600 choice: graph_indexed vendor: greptile # or coderabbit, qodo-merge-pro
example.note: 1.4M LOC + polyglot + weak boundaries → graph-indexed catches the cross-module breaks.

example.title: 30k LOC TS service — wrong graph-indexed choice
example.code: repo: loc: 30_000 languages: [typescript] module_boundaries: strong pr_feedback_target_s: 60 choice: graph_indexed vendor: coderabbit_pro
example.note: 30k LOC TS service: graph adds minutes of latency and reviewer cost for no measurable find-rate gain over Sourcery diff-only.

antipattern.description: Stacking both a graph-indexed reviewer AND a diff-only reviewer on every PR by default.
antipattern.reason: Doubles cost, doubles comment noise, halves human attention on the actual review. Use the graph-indexed bot only on demand (`/review`).

section.reference: Repo-property checklist that picks the architecture.

section.name: reviewer-matrix
section.p: Reads `git diff` plus the immediate file context. Sub-minute feedback, low cost, miss cross-module impact.

list.item: Sourcery — diff + Python/JS static analysis, mature suggestion patches.
GitHub Copilot Code Review — free tier, diff + nearest-neighbour file context.
Codeball — deep-learning classifier; emits `risk_score` 0..1 (auto-merge below 0.05 typical).
Qodo Merge `/review` (basic mode) — diff-only when run without graph indexing enabled.

rule.statement: When choosing a diff-only reviewer, pin a single vendor per repo and set `auto_describe = true`, `auto_review = true`, `auto_improve = false`. Never enable two diff-only reviewers on the same PR.
rule.rationale: Two diff-only bots produce nearly identical comments — pure noise without coverage gain.

section.p: Builds a repo-wide knowledge graph (call sites, type flows, ownership). First index is minutes; PR-time queries trace ripple effects.

list.item: Greptile — repo graph + retrieval; flagship find: silent cross-package breakage on rename.
CodeRabbit Pro — graph + multi-agent verifier (`@coderabbitai` chat, `/resolve`, `/review`).
Qodo Merge 2.0 — multi-agent + PR-history context (Feb 2026 release).

rule.statement: When choosing a graph-indexed reviewer, budget for the index refresh (cron rebuild on `main` push) and configure ownership rules so the bot tags the right CODEOWNER for each file the ripple touches. Disable `/improve`-style auto-suggestion patches by default — graph-indexed comments are dense enough.
rule.rationale: Stale graph misses recent renames; graph-indexed comment noise plus suggestion-patch noise overwhelms human reviewers and erodes trust.

template.purpose: Repo-property checklist that picks diff-only vs graph-indexed.
